nodes.description: The main product surface is a single Next.js workspace with three views: notes, inspect, and project. It coordinates repository selection, loading state, canvas state, graph inspection, conversation history, and build/plan actions from one client entry point. The Home component wires startup refreshes and keeps the active repo, open tabs, and console thread synchronized.
nodes.title: Workspace Shell

nodes.description: Users work against one active repository at a time, with recent projects, sample repos, and workspace health shown before deeper actions. The backend persists the chosen repo and derives whether the workspace is effectively empty by counting visible project files and checking for existing canvas notes. This is the gate that decides whether the UI should prompt for initial canvas generation.
nodes.title: Project Context

nodes.description: Konceptura’s core ingestion workflow runs code-graph-rag locally against a selected repository and records the active repo plus job status in backend state. Clean indexing deletes the repository hash cache first, then launches the graph update process asynchronously. This gives the rest of the product a graph-backed view of symbols, structure, and dependencies.
nodes.title: Code Graph Indexing

nodes.description: The inspect surface exposes structure browsing, ranked symbol search, raw Cypher queries, and relationship traversal over the indexed graph. GraphService talks directly to Memgraph through the Neo4j driver and converts graph nodes, relationships, and paths into API-safe records. The frontend then lets users inspect either search results or structure tree nodes and fetch outgoing call relationships for context.
nodes.title: Graph Inspection

nodes.description: Before edits, the system can estimate likely affected symbols and files from a natural-language prompt. The backend scores matching symbols heuristically, then expands across CALLS and IMPORTS edges to build a prioritized file list with reasons. This gives users and Codex a practical change radius rather than a purely lexical search result.
nodes.title: Impact Analysis

nodes.description: The canvas is the editable product memory for architecture notes, with nodes for responsibilities and labeled edges for relationships. Notes carry titles, descriptions, tags, linked files, and linked symbols, and are stored per target repository under `.konceptura/canvas.json`. The frontend renders them on a zoomable React Flow board, while the backend owns persistence, validation, and edge cleanup when notes are removed.
nodes.title: Architecture Canvas

nodes.description: When a repo has files but no notes, Konceptura can auto-map the codebase into architecture notes for the workspace. The backend asks Codex to inspect the repository and return structured JSON notes and edges, then merges only non-duplicate titles into the stored canvas and summarizes what changed. Positioning logic groups generated notes by tag families so the initial canvas is readable rather than random.
nodes.title: Canvas Generation

nodes.description: The main execution workflow lets the user either ask for a plan or request direct implementation in the active repository. The backend can generate a plan-only response, or run Codex with semantic workspace context, graph-derived impact context, and conversation context, then diff the repository snapshot to report changed files. After a build, the system also refreshes architecture notes so the canvas stays aligned with the codebase.
nodes.title: AI Build Loop

nodes.description: Each repository has its own persistent conversation thread list stored inside `.konceptura/conversations.json`. The UI appends user prompts, plan outputs, build summaries, and errors into that thread so implementation sessions can continue naturally. This memory is also fed back into planning and build requests as conversation context.
nodes.title: Conversation Memory

nodes.description: Konceptura includes two lightweight governance surfaces around AI-driven work: editable `AGENTS.md` instructions for the target repo and git commit assistance after changes exist. The backend reads and writes the agents document, detects git status, and asks Codex for a suggested conventional commit message based on changed files and diff stats. The UI exposes both as part of the active project workflow rather than separate tooling.
nodes.title: Repo Governance

nodes.description: A FastAPI backend is the coordination boundary between the UI and local tooling. It exposes endpoints for indexing, graph queries, impact analysis, project state, conversations, canvas editing, planning, building, commits, and agents-document management, while also applying CORS for the frontend origin. Most product workflows are thin HTTP orchestration over the project, canvas, graph, and Codex services.
nodes.title: Backend API

nodes.description: The system is designed for self-hosted local execution rather than a remote SaaS backend. Memgraph runs in Docker, code-graph-rag and Codex are discovered from local binaries, and `konceptura.toml` defines repo paths, service paths, origins, and defaults. This runtime boundary explains why the app can inspect and modify local repositories directly.
nodes.title: Local Runtime

nodes.description: This responsibility tracks which repository the workspace is pointed at and keeps a short recent-project history so the app can reopen work quickly. It also reports whether a repo has visible files and whether notes already exist, which drives empty-state behavior in the UI. The evidence is the persisted project profile and workspace-status logic in the backend.
nodes.title: Workspace Profile

nodes.description: This workflow turns a selected repository into a graph-backed representation using the local code-graph tool. It validates the repo path, supports dry-run command previews, records active repo state, and sets the current canvas repo once indexing begins. The business effect is that later search, structure, and impact features all work against the indexed project.
nodes.title: Repository Indexing

nodes.description: This boundary owns direct communication with Memgraph and exposes reusable graph primitives to the rest of the backend. It lists searchable symbols, runs raw Cypher, resolves relationships, and builds structure trees from graph data. The implementation is centered on a Neo4j-compatible driver and small ranking utilities for symbol search.
nodes.title: Code Graph Access

nodes.description: This boundary delegates repository work to the local Codex CLI and turns the run into a structured response with summaries, command traces, and file diffs. It can enrich prompts with semantic workspace context and graph-derived impact hints, and it exposes separate paths for build, ask, and plan behavior. The implementation snapshots the repo before and after runs and supports bypassing the sandbox by configuration.
nodes.title: Codex Edit Runner

nodes.description: This workflow converts a repository and user prompt into structured canvas notes and relationship edges instead of freeform text. It uses Codex to return strict JSON, then falls back to prompt-only notes if Codex is unavailable. The business purpose is to keep an editable architectural map alongside code changes.
nodes.title: Architecture Note Generation

nodes.description: This stateful area stores editable architecture notes per repository under `.konceptura/canvas.json` while also keeping a pointer document in backend state. It supports manual create, update, delete, reset, and append-generated-map operations, and prevents duplicate titles and edges when adding generated notes. The result is a persistent project-specific architecture canvas rather than a transient view.
nodes.title: Canvas Persistence

nodes.description: This orchestration flow is the product's main closed loop: edit the repo with Codex, regenerate architecture notes, append them to the canvas, and summarize both code and note changes. It exists in both request-response and streamed forms so the UI can show progress while work is happening. This is the clearest expression of the app's promise to keep implementation and architecture notes in sync.
nodes.title: Build And Refresh Loop

nodes.description: This responsibility stores project-scoped chat history in `.konceptura/conversations.json` and exposes CRUD endpoints for named conversations. The frontend uses it to preserve build, ask, and planning context over time instead of treating each Codex run as stateless. The implementation sorts conversations by most recent activity and seeds a placeholder Main thread when nothing exists yet.
nodes.title: Project Conversations

nodes.description: This area gives the workspace lightweight project controls beyond editing code: AGENTS.md management, git dirty-state inspection, commit creation, and commit-message suggestion. It turns repository hygiene into an explicit UI workflow rather than leaving it entirely to the terminal. The backend ties git status to Codex-generated suggested commit messages when changes exist.
nodes.title: Project Governance

nodes.description: This UI surface renders the architecture notes as draggable nodes on a visual board and exposes direct manipulation behaviors like create-on-double-click, selection, open-on-double-click, context menus, and persisted viewport state. It intentionally filters visible edges around the selected node to keep the map legible. The implementation is built on React Flow with note-specific node rendering.
nodes.title: Canvas Board

nodes.description: This UI surface turns the code graph into browsable developer views: structure tree, ranked search, and relationship inspection. It lets the user move from a project-wide structural overview to symbol-level edges and impact reasoning without leaving the workspace. The tree component itself is simple, while the page component coordinates data loading and selected-node inspection.
nodes.title: Inspection Panel

nodes.description: The app treats repository selection as the top-level context boundary. Saving or opening a project resets canvas state, conversations, and workspace metadata, then reloads the current repo-specific assets and status. This is grounded in the project profile APIs and the frontend handlers that rehydrate the workspace when the repo changes.
nodes.title: Project Switching

nodes.description: The core product surface is the visual note canvas, where architecture notes become the editable working spec for a repository. Users can generate the map from a prompt, add notes manually, move them spatially, and navigate by opening individual notes as tabs. The main implementation evidence is the React Flow board plus the `/canvas` and `/canvas/generate` backend routes.
nodes.title: Canvas Specs Board

nodes.description: Notes are not only documentation; they are ranked and reused as semantic context for future planning, asking, and building. The frontend tracks visible, focused, and pinned notes, then composes a reduced note summary before sending prompts to the backend. That makes the canvas a context engine for vibecoding through specs rather than just a visualization layer.
nodes.title: Note Context Selection

nodes.description: The main business loop turns a natural-language request plus selected note context into a real coding run, then refreshes the canvas with new architecture notes. The streaming endpoint emits phases for building and note updates, and the frontend appends the results into the conversation console. This is the clearest expression of the product promise: build through specs, then fold the implementation back into the visual workspace.
nodes.title: Spec To Code Run

nodes.description: The app supports two lighter-weight modes around the main build flow: plan-first and ask-only. Planning produces an implementation outline that can later be approved into a real build, while ask mode answers codebase questions without changing files. This reinforces a conversational spec workflow, although it also adds more surfaces beyond the core canvas-to-build experience.
nodes.title: Plan And Ask Loop

nodes.description: All implementation, planning, question answering, architecture-note generation, and commit-message suggestion are funneled through a backend `CodexService`. It composes prompt context from the graph and selected notes, shells out to the Codex CLI, snapshots repo diffs, and parses JSONL events back into app responses. This is the main integration boundary between the visual workspace and actual code changes.
nodes.title: Codex Execution Boundary

nodes.description: The workspace includes adjacent delivery controls for editing `AGENTS.md`, checking git status, suggesting commit messages, and creating commits from the UI. These features help operationalize generated changes, but they also push the product toward a general coding workstation. If the target is a tighter visual vibecoding app through specs, this area is a plausible simplification candidate.
nodes.title: Delivery Controls
